title: Matrix Product Operator Encodings of the Magnus Expansion and Dyson Series
authors: Victor Vanthilt, Maarten Van Damme, Jutho Haegeman, Ian P. McCulloch, Laurens Vanderstraeten
abstract: We introduce a matrix product operator (MPO) encoding of the Magnus expansion and the Dyson series for one-dimensional quantum lattice models with time-dependent Hamiltonians. The MPO construction can be made accurate up to arbitrary order in the time step, it can be applied to both finite and infinite systems, and it can handle long-range interactions. The resulting MPO can be combined with state-of-the-art time evolution algorithms based on matrix product states, allowing for drastic improvements in simulating evolution under time-dependent Hamiltonians. Our MPO construction can also be used for the optimization of quantum circuits in the context of quantum simulation of time-dependent Hamiltonians.
summary: The paper introduces efficient MPO encodings for the Magnus expansion and Dyson series to simulate time-dependent quantum lattice models. By using novel compression and rewiring techniques, the authors achieve high-order accuracy with manageable bond dimensions, outperforming standard methods like TDVP in the runtime-accuracy trade-off.
main_problem: Efficiently simulating the real-time evolution of 1D quantum many-body systems under time-dependent Hamiltonians, specifically addressing the exponential growth of bond dimensions and the complexity of time-ordered expansions.
main_result: The authors develop new Matrix Product Operator (MPO) encodings for the Magnus expansion and Dyson series that are size-extensive, handle long-range interactions, and achieve an error scaling of O(dt^N).
method: The method uses tensor network formalisms (MPS/MPO) to encode expansions, employing a 'rewiring' technique for the Dyson series and a 'row compression' algorithm to significantly reduce the MPO bond dimension.
model_or_system: One-dimensional or quasi-1D quantum lattice models, including spin chains (e.g., Heisenberg XXZ) and models with time-dependent, non-commuting Hamiltonians.
key_observables: Trace distance (for finite systems) and a modified trace distance (for the thermodynamic limit) to measure error relative to reference states.
important_parameters: Bond dimension (D or chi), expansion order (N), time step (dt), and the interaction range (short-range to long-range).
main_assumptions: The construction assumes injective MPS for uniqueness in the thermodynamic limit and relies on the truncation of MPO bond dimensions during compression.
figures_summary: Figure 1 and 2 show error scaling of O(dt^N) for finite and infinite systems; Figure 3 demonstrates the runtime-accuracy trade-off advantage over TDVP; Figure 4 shows high-accuracy benchmarks for the 4th-order Dyson MPO.
paper_structure: The paper begins with notation and a recap of time-independent MPO construction, followed by the Magnus expansion approach, the Dyson series approach, MPO compression techniques, and finally numerical benchmarking.
why_interesting: This is highly relevant for many-body dynamics as it provides a systematic, high-order method to simulate driven quantum systems (like Floquet systems) with much better efficiency than traditional time-stepping methods.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Benchmark for finite-system size. We plot the error measure ε [Eq. (79)] of the state evolved with the Dyson-MPO of different orders with respect to a quasi-exact simulation. We evolve the state |0⟩⊗L over one period of the Hamiltonian of Eq. (78). The quasi-exact simulation was generated with QuantumOptics.jl as a function of the time step.

FIG. 2. Benchmark for infinite-size system. We plot the error measure ε [Eq. (82)] of the state evolved with the Dyson-MPO of different orders, with respect to a TDVP simulation. We evolve the N´eel state over one period of the Hamiltonian of Eq. (80). The infinite-size TDVP simulation is performed with a time step of dt = 10−7.
FIG. 3. Runtime comparison. We compare the total runtime for the results of Fig. 2 as a function of the achieved accuracy.
FIG. 4. Finite-size benchmark (left) and infinite-size benchmark (right) using the most accurate wavefunction generated with the 4th order Dyson MPO with the smallest timestep as reference.
